title: Risk Register & FMEA
method: FMEA. RPN = Severity x Likelihood x Detectability, each 1-10. Higher RPN = higher priority. Detectability: 1 = certain to be caught before harm, 10 = undetectable until harm occurs.
note: Scores are the authors' engineering judgement, not measured failure rates. They are a PRIORITISATION tool, not a probability claim. Recompute after every design change and after every real incident. The value is in the ranking, not the absolute numbers.
the_philosophy: The risks that kill ventures are rarely the dramatic ones. They are the ones with high SEVERITY and high DETECTABILITY-difficulty — the failures nobody sees coming until the harm is done. This register is sorted to surface those, not the obvious ones.
the_phased_scope: This register now spans all three phases. R-01 through R-15 are the Phase 3 hardware/field FMEA and are unchanged. R-16 through R-18 are Phase 1/2 software and business risks, scored with the same method and the same discipline — an untested freemium funnel gets named as bluntly as an untested winch.

risks.description: Sensor fouling produces plausible but wrong readings; a farmer acts on bad data.
risks.cause: Biofilm drift on optical/electrode sensors, undetected between reference checks.
risks.effect: False 'all clear' or false alarm. A false all-clear that precedes a real kill is the worst outcome the product can produce — we told them they were safe.
risks.mitigation: Weekly reference check (the only real control); flat-line and climatology QC tests; drift detection; dry-dock storage that reduces fouling versus a moored buoy.
risks.note: HIGHEST RPN in the register, and it is not a dramatic failure — it is a silent one. This is the risk that most deserves attention and least attracts it. A monitoring product's deepest liability is confidently wrong data.

risks.description: Unvalidated kill-risk score produces a false negative; farmer defers action and loses stock.
risks.cause: Shipping a predictive model before validation under commercial pressure.
risks.effect: We convert the farmer's cautious uncertainty into false confidence, and they act on it. Direct harm caused by us.
risks.mitigation: Do not ship the score until validated. Show the mechanism in plain language instead. Hard organisational rule that the scientist has veto over predictive claims.
risks.note: The mitigation is organisational discipline, not technology, which makes it fragile — it must survive the commercial pressure documented in ai-ml-models.json. Listed at severity 10 because a false negative here can cost a livelihood.

risks.description: CAAP declines or indefinitely delays BVLOS authorisation.
risks.cause: External regulatory decision outside our control.
risks.effect: The autonomous dawn-sortie use case — the flagship — becomes illegal or requires a pilot at 04:30, destroying the unit economics.
risks.mitigation: Engage CAAP from Phase 0. Build the safety case early. Stress-test the business model against VLOS-only economics so BVLOS is upside, not a load-bearing assumption.
risks.note: Detectability is low (3) — we will see this coming, which is the one mercy. But it gates the margin, so it is on the critical path regardless.

risks.description: Target operators will not pay at any price we can offer.
risks.cause: Most operators currently spend ~zero on monitoring; we are creating a budget line, not taking share.
risks.effect: No revenue. The venture fails regardless of technical success.
risks.mitigation: Phase 0 farmer discovery with real willingness-to-pay testing (paid pilots, not survey questions). Lead with the checkable aeration-cost ROI. Cooperative and financing channels for smallholders.
risks.note: This is a market risk, not a technical one, and it is the one most likely to actually kill the company. It is detectable early (Phase 0) if the team is honest in discovery — which is why the Phase 0 gate has a kill criterion for it.

risks.description: Aircraft with deployed probe snags on cage structure over people.
risks.cause: Submerged, invisible netting and mooring lines.
risks.effect: Uncontrolled aircraft directly above a working cage where people are present. Potential injury.
risks.mitigation: Tension anomaly detection; slack-and-retry; tether cutter; shrouded rotors; station siting away from structure; no-profile zones; operator ground-clearance during sorties.
risks.note: The shrouds (which cost mass and endurance) pay for themselves here. This is the risk that justifies not cutting them.

risks.description: Aircraft ditches; lithium pack contaminates a food-production water body.
risks.cause: Motor failure, severe weather, winch entanglement, or link loss over water.
risks.effect: Environmental incident in the water the customer farms. Reputational and regulatory harm beyond the lost asset.
risks.mitigation: Positive buoyancy; lithium containment; locator beacon; water-aware failsafe; consider hexarotor for motor-out survivability.

risks.description: Autonomous or erroneous dispensing over-doses a water body.
risks.cause: Model error, sensor error, or software fault triggering discharge.
risks.effect: Chemical harm to stock. A poisoned pond.
risks.mitigation: No autonomous dispensing, ever. Per-mission human authorisation. Firmware dose ceiling not overridable from the dashboard. Mechanical interlock. Load-cell logging.
risks.note: Low likelihood BECAUSE of the hard-coded controls. Remove any one of them and the likelihood jumps. The controls are the reason this RPN is low, not an inherent property of the system.

risks.description: Loss of social licence in a lake community.
risks.cause: Perceived surveillance, a crash, a wildlife incident, or simply deploying without community engagement.
risks.effect: The community makes operation socially impossible. In a lake town, this is decided over months of conversation and is nearly irreversible once lost.
risks.mitigation: Community notification at commissioning; no person-detection; no IUU-enforcement use; transparent purpose; benign design language; local hiring.
risks.note: High RPN and hard to detect until it is too late. Social licence is not recoverable at the rate it is lost. Under-weighted by technical teams because it is not an engineering problem.

risks.description: Tethered-probe pendulum instability makes the aircraft uncontrollable in useful wind.
risks.cause: Swinging pendulum mass couples into the flight controller as an unmodeled disturbance; natural frequency shifts with tether length.
risks.effect: The real wind limit collapses; mission profiles fail; the platform becomes fair-weather only.
risks.mitigation: Pendulum feedforward control; tether damping; flight-test characterisation early; accept a lower wind limit if necessary.
risks.note: The single largest technical unknown. Detectable early (Phase 2 flight test) — which is why the winch and pendulum work is on the critical path.

risks.description: Mass budget does not close; the aircraft cannot carry the mission.
risks.cause: Component masses exceed the paper targets, especially the sonde and winch.
risks.effect: The concept as described is not buildable on a viable multirotor.
risks.mitigation: Per-mission payload configuration; reposition dispensing as treatment-only; accept a heavier MTOM bracket; descope thermal. This is Phase 0 paper arithmetic — resolvable before spending on hardware.
risks.note: Low detectability difficulty (2) is the good news: it is arithmetic available today. The mass budget in omnidrone-specifications.json already shows it does not close fully-configured, which is why per-mission configuration is baseline rather than optimisation.

risks.description: Weather grounds the aircraft exactly when risk peaks.
risks.cause: Monsoon storms trigger overturn events AND exceed the flight envelope.
risks.effect: The catastrophe-prevention claim is undercut — we cannot fly during the events we sell against.
risks.mitigation: Reposition as chronic-management + pre-event measurement (measure the loaded gun before the storm). Fixed-buoy hybrid for continuity through weather. Simulation core to project through the gap.
risks.note: High likelihood (8) — this is not a maybe, it is a certainty. The mitigation is honest positioning, not engineering. Trying to sell around it rather than reposition around it is the trap.

risks.cause: Technician perceives the system as surveillance of their performance; stops charging, cleaning, reporting faithfully.
risks.effect: Silent data degradation; the deployment fails and nobody knows why.
risks.mitigation: Never surface a technician-performance metric. Frame the system as the technician's instrument. Local hiring and training. Include them in the value story.
risks.note: High RPN driven by detectability — a disengaged technician does not file a ticket. This is a design-and-culture control, and it must survive an owner asking for the performance dashboard. See customer-personas.json.

risks.description: Toxicity overclaim leads to consumption of contaminated product.
risks.cause: UI or marketing blurs the pigment-vs-toxin distinction; operator reads low chlorophyll as 'safe to harvest'.
risks.effect: Food safety incident. People eat contaminated shellfish or fish.
risks.mitigation: Hard rule: never display a toxicity indicator. Chlorophyll and phycocyanin always framed as 'sample the water', never as clearance. Recommend lab assay on elevated readings.
risks.note: Low likelihood only because of disciplined framing. The severity is maximal because the harm is to a consumer's health, not a customer's margin.

risks.description: Unit economics do not close; cost to serve exceeds willingness to pay.
risks.cause: Underestimated maintenance labour, per-site sonde cost, dock cost, battery opex, and dispersed geography.
risks.effect: Negative contribution margin at scale. Growth accelerates losses.
risks.mitigation: Real BOM quotes in Phase 0. Measured (not modeled) unit economics in the Phase 4 pilot. Density-based site qualification. Cooperative amortisation.
risks.note: See unit-economics.json, which refuses to state a number precisely because this risk is unquantified. High RPN and it hides inside a spreadsheet that looks fine until the assumptions are tested.

risks.description: Enabling over-stocking — the product makes the underlying problem worse.
risks.cause: Better instruments let operators push density closer to the edge.
risks.effect: Higher stocking, higher systemic risk, and complicity in the very problem we sell against.
risks.mitigation: Carrying-capacity analytics that surface the systemic cost; cooperative/regulator framing; not marketing the product as a licence to intensify.
risks.note: An ethical risk that is also a real second-order effect. Deserves the thesis ethics chapter. Under-detectable because it manifests as customer 'success' (higher stocking) before it manifests as harm.

risks.description: Phase 1 app activation rate is too low — installs do not convert into a completed farm profile or a first logged reading.
risks.cause: Onboarding friction, unclear value before enough data has accumulated to show anything back to the user, uneven digital literacy and connectivity among smallholder segments.
risks.effect: The acquisition engine fills the top of the funnel but not the middle; CAC is spent without an activated base, and there is no user base to feed the Phase 2 conversion funnel or the Phase 2 ML models.
risks.mitigation: Guided, short farm-setup wizard; show a useful result (even a generic one) before asking for the first real log; community-led onboarding through existing fish-farming groups rather than cold acquisition; track activation as a first-class metric from day one.
risks.note: Low detectability difficulty (2) is the good news — activation is a directly measured funnel metric, visible immediately, unlike the silent hardware failures above. The risk is real but at least it cannot hide.

risks.description: Free-tier farmers churn before converting to Phase 2 paid tiers or before enough longitudinal data accumulates to make the free tier itself useful.
risks.cause: No sustained logging habit forms; a farm's production cycle runs months, which is longer than most consumer apps' engagement window; free substitutes (a notebook, a group chat) are 'good enough' for a beginner.
risks.effect: Undercuts both revenue (no conversion to Premium/marketplace/consultations) and the Phase 2 data asset — predictive health models need the longitudinal logs that churned users never produced.
risks.mitigation: Habit-forming cadence (feeding/logging reminders tied to real husbandry tasks, not notifications for their own sake); visible personal value (trend charts on the farmer's own data) before any upsell; community features that create a reason to return independent of the core logging task.
risks.note: This is the freemium-model equivalent of R-04 — untested willingness to stay, not just willingness to pay. Detectable via cohort retention curves early, which is why instrumenting retention from the first Phase 1 release matters.

risks.description: Self-reported Phase 1 water-quality and farm logs (manual/photo entry, no sensor verification) are inaccurate, inconsistent, rounded, or gamed, and Phase 2 predictive models are trained on this base.
risks.cause: No hardware verification exists at Phase 1; farmers may mis-measure, estimate, forget entries, or enter optimistic values, and there is no reference check analogous to the weekly sonde calibration that catches drift in Phase 3.
risks.effect: Phase 2 predictive health and analytics models inherit noisy or biased training data. This is the software-era version of R-01: a plausible but wrong number that someone acts on, except here there is no dedicated sonde and no reference-check control to catch it.
risks.mitigation: Plausibility and range checks on entry; gentle prompts rather than blocking; cross-reference against community/regional benchmarks to flag outliers; photo-based entries verified where possible by the same disease-ID computer vision already in the product; when a farm graduates to Phase 3 sensors, use the overlap period to calibrate and score the reliability of that farm's prior self-reports rather than trusting them uniformly.
risks.note: SECOND-HIGHEST raw RPN in the register, and it is R-01's direct software analogue — the philosophy above (silent data corruption over dramatic failure) applies just as much to a self-reported log as to a fouled sensor. There is no equivalent of the reference check here yet; this is an open control gap, not a solved one.

top_5_by_residual_rpn.risk: Self-reported Phase 1 logs → confidently wrong training data
Silent sensor drift → confidently wrong data

risk_summary.the_pattern: The top residual risks are NOT the dramatic ones (crash, poisoning). They are silent data corruption, commercial non-viability, and second-order harms — and the pattern now holds across the whole platform, not just the hardware: R-18 (unverified self-reported farm logs) is the Phase 1/2 software mirror of R-01 (drifted sensors), and it now outranks it. Dramatic risks attract engineering attention and get mitigated hard; quiet risks do not, so their residual scores stay high. A team that only worries about crashes — or only about churn — is worrying about the wrong things.

how_to_use_this_register: In the pitch: show R-04, R-14, and R-03 unprompted. Volunteering your top commercial and regulatory risks with mitigations is more convincing than a slide claiming there are none.
In the thesis: this is the limitations-and-risk chapter, and R-15 anchors the ethics discussion.
In engineering: the residual RPN ranking is the priority order. Work the top of the list.
Recompute after every real incident. A register that never changes is a register nobody uses.
